title: Ships First, Then Shore. Why Small Modular Reactors May Find… | by Patrick Gros | Medium
text:
Ships First, Then Shore
Why Small Modular Reactors May Find Their First Commercial Success at Sea
Introduction: The Wrong Target, The Right Solution
The nuclear industry and governments worldwide are pouring billions into land-based Small Modular Reactor (SMR) deployment. The Trump administration has announced a goal of 400 GW of nuclear capacity. The United Kingdom has pledged £2.5 billion. Tech giants Amazon and Google have committed hundreds of millions to nuclear power for their data centers. Yet after decades of promises, not a single commercial SMR operates in the United States, and the most advanced American project — NuScale’s Carbon Free Power Project — was cancelled in November 2023 after costs escalated from $3 billion to $9.3 billion.
Meanwhile, a different challenge demands attention. Eighty-five percent of the one billion tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions that the maritime industry must eliminate by 2050 comes from deep-sea vessels exceeding five megawatts of power. The global shipping fleet consumes approximately 330 million metric tonnes of fuel annually — more than the world’s entire jet fuel consumption. Container shipping alone emitted a record 240.6 million tonnes of CO₂ in 2024, a fourteen percent increase over the previous year. And unlike aviation, shipping has no viable zero-carbon alternative fuel that can be produced at scale without diverting the green electricity desperately needed to decarbonize power grids on land.
What if the nuclear industry has been aiming at the wrong target?
Let’s advance a provocative thesis: Small modular reactors face structural impediments to terrestrial deployment that may be largely circumvented — or even converted to advantages — in maritime applications. Commercial shipping represents not merely an alternative market for SMRs, but potentially the optimal first-deployment environment. Maritime SMRs offer a pathway to prove the technology, establish supply chains, achieve the manufacturing scale necessary for cost reduction, and create the economic conditions for eventual terrestrial success. For the United States specifically, maritime nuclear propulsion aligns with an urgent national security imperative: the revitalization of a merchant fleet that has dwindled to a mere 180 vessels — 0.4 percent of the global total — while China commands over 5,500 ships and forty-seven percent of global shipbuilding capacity.
The case proceeds in five parts. First, I examine the multifaceted barriers to immediate terrestrial SMR deployment — barriers that explain why no commercial SMR operates in the United States despite decades of development. Second, I document the environmental imperative in commercial shipping, where emissions are rising and alternative fuels face fundamental limitations. Third, I present the operational and economic case for maritime SMRs, drawing on seventy years of naval nuclear experience and emerging industry momentum. Fourth, I address America’s critical need for merchant fleet expansion, a national security crisis hiding in plain sight. Finally, I consider the competitive implications of a global race already underway, as South Korea, China, and European consortia move toward nuclear-powered commercial vessels.
Part I: The Case Against Immediate Terrestrial SMR Deployment
The enthusiasm for terrestrial SMRs is understandable. Smaller reactors promise factory fabrication, modular construction, reduced capital requirements, and deployment flexibility impossible with gigawatt-scale nuclear plants. The theory is elegant: standardized designs manufactured in controlled factory environments should follow the cost curves of other manufactured goods — solar panels, wind turbines, batteries — rather than the distressing pattern of nuclear construction, where costs have historically increased with each successive plant.
The reality, however, has proven stubborn.
The Economic Reality Check
NuScale Power’s Carbon Free Power Project was, until its cancellation, the most advanced SMR project in the United States. It was also a case study in cost escalation. Initial estimates in 2015 projected a total cost of $3 billion for the twelve-module facility. By 2023, that figure had ballooned to $9.3 billion — a 210 percent increase. The projected levelized cost of electricity rose from $55 per megawatt-hour to $89, a sixty-two percent overage that made the project uncompetitive with alternatives. Seventy-four percent of the municipal utilities in the Utah Associated Municipal Power Systems declined to subscribe to the project’s output at those prices. The project was terminated.
NuScale’s experience is not anomalous. The International Energy Agency estimates SMR overnight costs at approximately $10,000 per kilowatt of capacity, compared to roughly $6,600 per kilowatt for traditional large-scale nuclear plants. The Energy Information Administration projects advanced nuclear levelized costs at $95–110 per megawatt-hour. These figures contrast unfavorably with solar-plus-storage systems, which the same agency estimates at $46–102 per megawatt-hour, with costs continuing to decline.
The problem compounds when one considers the competitive landscape. The International Renewable Energy Agency reports that ninety-one percent of new renewable energy projects commissioned in 2024 produced electricity more cheaply than fossil fuel alternatives. Eighty-seven percent of all new global power capacity added in 2024 was renewable. Battery storage costs have fallen more than fifty percent in the past decade. Solar and wind projects deploy in one to three years; SMRs, even optimistically, require seven to fifteen years from commitment to commercial operation.
This creates what economists call an opportunity cost problem. Every dollar invested in SMR development is a dollar not invested in technologies that are cheaper, faster to deploy, and declining in cost. For utilities and ratepayers focused on near-term decarbonization, the calculus does not favor nuclear.
The Regulatory and Licensing Barriers
Even if SMRs could compete on cost, they face regulatory timelines incompatible with climate urgency. The most optimistic projections place commercial SMR deployment in the early 2030s. This means SMRs cannot contribute meaningfully to 2030 emissions targets and will struggle to impact 2035 goals. For policymakers operating under the Paris Agreement’s ratcheting ambitions, this timeline mismatch is disqualifying.
Each terrestrial installation, moreover, requires site-specific environmental impact assessments, local permitting, community engagement, and political approval. The distributed nature of SMR deployment — the very feature that advocates tout as an advantage — multiplies these regulatory touchpoints. As one analysis noted, meaningful SMR deployment “would require hundreds of small reactors around the world, each hosted by a local community.” This represents, in the analysts’ understated phrasing, a “formidable challenge.”
The NIMBY phenomenon that has delayed large nuclear projects does not disappear with smaller reactors; it simply multiplies across more sites. Every community hosting an SMR becomes a potential battleground for anti-nuclear activism, regardless of the actual risk profile of modern reactor designs.
Fuel Supply Constraints
Many advanced SMR designs require High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium (HALEU), a fuel form not widely produced for commercial purposes. Currently, only the United States and Russia manufacture HALEU commercially, and Russia dominates the market. The U.S. nuclear industry has warned that deployment of certain SMR designs could be delayed by years due to limited HALEU availability. European enrichment companies have indicated they lack the business case to develop HALEU production capacity without guaranteed demand — but demand cannot materialize without available fuel.
This chicken-and-egg problem pervades SMR development. Manufacturing supply chains will not develop without demand; demand cannot materialize without demonstrated economics; demonstrated economics require manufacturing scale; manufacturing scale requires supply chains. Breaking this cycle requires either massive government intervention or an alternative pathway to initial deployment.
Waste Management Uncertainty
A 2022 study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences found that SMRs may increase nuclear waste volumes by factors of two to thirty, depending on reactor design. Smaller reactor cores experience higher neutron leakage, reducing fuel efficiency and creating waste streams that are more chemically and physically reactive than those from conventional reactors. The United States still lacks a permanent geological repository for existing nuclear waste; adding distributed SMR waste sites compounds a problem already decades from resolution.
The Summary Challenge
These barriers — economic, regulatory, fuel supply, waste management, public acceptance — are formidable. They explain why, despite decades of development and billions in investment, commercial SMRs remain aspirational rather than operational in the United States. But it is essential to recognize that these barriers are not inherent to SMR technology. They are specific to terrestrial deployment. Maritime applications fundamentally alter this calculus.
